quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 15 Quiz 5
quiz.subtitle: 15.5: Identifying Conic Sections from General Form
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Classifying conics using coefficients A and C

questions.question: Identify the conic section: $3x^2 + 3y^2 - 6x + 4y - 10 = 0$
questions.explanation: Coefficients of $x^2$ and $y^2$ are both $3$ (equal) → Circle.

questions.question: Identify the conic section: $y^2 - 4x + 2y + 5 = 0$
questions.explanation: Only $y$ is squared; there is no $x^2$ term. This is a parabola.

questions.question: Identify the conic section: $4x^2 - 9y^2 + 32x - 18y + 37 = 0$
questions.explanation: Coefficients $4$ and $-9$ have opposite signs → Hyperbola.

questions.question: Identify the conic section: $2x^2 + 5y^2 + 8x - 20y + 1 = 0$
questions.explanation: Coefficients $2$ and $5$ are different but both positive → Ellipse.

questions.question: For $9x^2 + 4y^2 - 36x + 24y + 36 = 0$, use the discriminant $B^2 - 4AC$ to identify the conic.
For $2x^2 - 3y^2 + 8x - 6y - 5 = 0$, use the discriminant to identify the conic.
Which conic is represented by $x^2 + y^2 = 0$?
questions.explanation: Only $(0,0)$ satisfies the equation. This is a degenerate circle — a single point.

questions.question: Identify the conic: $x^2 - y^2 = 0$
questions.explanation: Factor: $(x-y)(x+y)=0$, which represents the lines $y=x$ and $y=-x$.

questions.question: Identify the conic: $16x^2 + 16y^2 + 96x - 64y + 1 = 0$
questions.explanation: $A = C = 16$. Equal coefficients on $x^2$ and $y^2$ → Circle.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can spot a conic from a mile away — using coefficients, the discriminant, and recognizing degenerate cases.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Key reminders: (1) $A=C$ → Circle; $A$ and $C$ same sign but unequal → Ellipse, (2) $A$ and $C$ opposite signs → Hyperbola, (3) Only one squared term → Parabola, (4) $B^2-4AC$: negative = ellipse/circle, zero = parabola, positive = hyperbola.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: (1) Checking whether both variables are squared, (2) Comparing the signs and values of $A$ and $C$, (3) Using the discriminant when coefficients aren't obvious, (4) Degenerate cases: $r^2=0$ → point; $r^2<0$ → no graph; difference of squares = 0 → intersecting lines.
scoreMessages.0: Review identifying conics from $Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0$: (1) Only one squared term ($A=0$ or $C=0$) → Parabola, (2) $A=C$ → Circle, (3) $A$ and $C$ same sign, $A \neq C$ → Ellipse, (4) $A$ and $C$ opposite signs → Hyperbola, (5) Discriminant $B^2-4AC$ confirms: negative = ellipse/circle, zero = parabola, positive = hyperbola.
